response:
# Government Benefits Portal — Pre-VPAT Accessibility Audit Plan

> **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol (audit-scope adaptation). Review with a11y-critic at each phase gate.
> **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA (mapped to Revised Section 508 / ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508)
> **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (desktop + mobile), keyboard-only users, voice-input users (Dragon), low-vision users, cognitive/low-literacy users (benefits applicants are a high-stakes, low-margin-for-error population)
> **Assistive technologies:** NVDA (Windows/Firefox), JAWS (Windows/Chrome), VoiceOver (macOS/Safari), iOS VoiceOver (Safari), TalkBack (Android/Chrome), keyboard-only, Dragon NaturallySpeaking

**Feature:** Systematic pre-VPAT accessibility audit of a 20-page government benefits portal, executable by a 2-engineer QA team in 3 months, producing a defensible ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508 conformance report.
**Risk Level:** High — legal/compliance deliverable (VPAT), high-stakes user population (public assistance applicants), authenticated transactional flows where a blocked journey means a citizen cannot apply for benefits.
**Component Type:** Site-level audit (not a single component). Methodology + sampling + tooling + reporting plan.

---

## Why This Is an Audit Plan, Not a Component Design Plan

This fixture routes through the a11y-planner, but the deliverable it asks for is a **testing/audit methodology**, not an accessible-implementation design for one widget. I am adapting the 9-phase planner protocol accordingly:

- The planner's **Phase 8 (Testing Strategy)** is the spine of this document, expanded into a full WCAG-EM evaluation.
- The planner's structural/interaction/focus/state/visual/content phases (2–7) become a **shared-component reference checklist** the auditors apply when they reach each pattern — so the audit isn't "look for problems" but "verify against a known-correct spec per APG."
- The planner's **review-checkpoint** discipline becomes the a11y-critic gates between audit phases.

This is the honest framing. A plan that pretended a 20-page site is one component would be dead on arrival. The value here is the **methodology** (what to test, in what order, with what tool, to what conformance standard) plus the **per-pattern correctness specs** that turn subjective "feels broken" judgments into citable findings.

The governing external standard is **WCAG-EM 1.0 (Website Accessibility Conformance Evaluation Methodology, W3C Working Group Note, 2014)** — https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG-EM/. WCAG-EM is the W3C's own procedure for evaluating whether a *website* (not a component) conforms, and it is the framework a VPAT's "Evaluation Methods Used" section should cite. Every sampling and reporting decision below traces to a WCAG-EM step.

---

## Scope & Context

**What:** Evaluate conformance of 20 pages + 8 shared patterns + 3 third-party integrations against WCAG 2.2 AA, and produce an ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508 with per-criterion conformance claims.

**Why:** Legal/procurement requirement. A VPAT is the artifact a government buyer (or this agency's own compliance office) relies on to assert Section 508 conformance. An inaccurate VPAT is worse than none — it creates legal exposure. The audit exists to make the VPAT's claims true and defensible.

**Who needs accessibility:** All five WCAG 2.2 disability categories, but with population-specific weighting. Public-assistance applicants skew toward:
- Higher disability prevalence than the general population (disability is itself a qualifying condition for many benefits).
- High mobile usage and mobile-only households — so **mobile screen readers (iOS VoiceOver, TalkBack) are not optional**, contra the "below expectations" note in the fixture.
- Lower digital literacy and cognitive load tolerance — so error recovery (WCAG 3.3.1, 3.3.3, 3.3.4) and consistent navigation (3.2.3) carry outsized real-world weight even though they are not the "flashiest" criteria.

**Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA. Note WCAG 2.2 added criteria that 4-year-old sites almost never satisfy and that auditors must explicitly probe: **2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured (Minimum)** (AA — sticky headers/cookie banners hiding the focused element), **2.5.7 Dragging Movements** (AA), **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum)** (AA, 24×24 CSS px — note this is the WCAG 2.2 AA figure; the planner's 44×44 reference is the AAA/Apple HIG figure), **3.2.6 Consistent Help** (A), **3.3.7 Redundant Entry** (A — re-asking for data already provided in a 6-step form), **3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Minimum)** (AA — reCAPTCHA and any cognitive-test login implicate this). These six are the highest-yield "new since the site was built" findings.

**Assistive technologies:** See header. The SR matrix below assigns which SR covers which page type rather than testing every page on every SR (5 SRs × 20 pages = 100 combinations is infeasible for 2 engineers in 3 months).

**Risk level:** High (compliance deliverable + high-stakes population + transactional flows).

**Existing code:** Live, server-rendered HTML + progressive JS enhancement, no SPA framework. This matters: focus management bugs are *less* likely than in an SPA (full page loads reset focus naturally), but **partial JS enhancements** (live search results, in-page alerts, the timeout modal, the date picker, the mega-menu) are exactly where the SPA-class bugs hide. The audit must concentrate manual effort on the JS-enhanced islands, not the static prose.

**Constraints:**
- 3-month deadline, hard (VPAT publication date).
- 2 QA engineers with *basic* a11y knowledge — the plan must be executable without expert judgment at every step; expert judgment is concentrated at the a11y-critic gates.
- axe DevTools Enterprise already licensed.
- Government compliance documentation required (audit trail, reproducibility).
- Output: ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508.

**Constraint-driven consequence:** With ~480 person-hours total (2 engineers × ~12 weeks × ~20 productive hrs/wk on this), you cannot test everything to equal depth. The entire plan is an exercise in **spending the scarce manual/SR hours where blocked journeys live, and spending cheap automated hours everywhere.** That is the thesis.

---

## Phase 0 — WCAG-EM Setup (Steps 1–2): Scope & Sampling

WCAG-EM Step 1 ("Define the Evaluation Scope") and Step 3 ("Select a Representative Sample") govern this section. Step 2 ("Explore the Target Website") is a 1-day reconnaissance task assigned at kickoff.

### Page Sampling Strategy (WCAG-EM Step 3)

**Do not test all 20 pages equally.** WCAG-EM explicitly endorses sampling: test a "structured sample" of representative pages plus a "randomly selected sample," and test *all* pages that are part of a complete process (Step 3b mandates that every page in a multi-step process be included — you cannot sample a checkout/application flow, because a single inaccessible step blocks the whole journey under SC 2.4.3 and the WCAG "complete processes" conformance requirement).

This produces a **3-tier risk model**:

#### Tier 1 — CRITICAL PATH (test exhaustively, every page, all methods)
These are "complete processes" under WCAG-EM 3b and the WCAG conformance requirement #3 ("Complete Processes"). A failure here means a citizen cannot get benefits.

| # | Page / Flow | Why Tier 1 |
|---|-------------|------------|
| 1 | Account creation | Entry to the entire authenticated experience; complex form + likely reCAPTCHA-adjacent |
| 2 | Login | Gate to everything; reCAPTCHA v2 present → SC 3.3.8 risk |
| 3–8 | Multi-step application (6 steps) | The core transaction. **All 6 steps tested individually** — no sampling. Redundant entry (3.3.7), error recovery (3.3.1/3.3.3/3.3.4), focus on validation, progress communication |
| 9 | Document upload | File-upload a11y is a notorious failure class (drag-only = 2.5.7, status not announced = 4.1.3) |
| 10 | Application status dashboard | Data tables (1.3.1), dynamic status, the payoff page of the whole journey |
| 11 | Notification center | Live updates (4.1.3), likely a list/feed pattern |

#### Tier 2 — SHARED COMPONENTS (test once, deeply; fix propagates everywhere)
This is the audit's leverage. The 8 shared patterns appear on most of the 20 pages. **Test each shared pattern once, in isolation, against its APG correctness spec (Phase A–F below). One fix to the mega-menu remediates it on all 20 pages.** This is what makes a 20-page audit achievable in 3 months.

| # | Shared Pattern | Appears On | APG Pattern to Verify Against |
|---|----------------|------------|-------------------------------|
| S1 | Global nav + mega-menu | All 20 | Disclosure or Menu/Menubar (decision in Phase A) |
| S2 | Search with live results | Most | Combobox (autocomplete) + live region |
| S3 | In-page alerts/notifications | Most | Alert / Status (live regions) |
| S4 | Data tables (application history) | Dashboard, history | Table semantics (no APG widget; HTML table correctness) |
| S5 | File upload | Upload, application | Native input + status messaging |
| S6 | Date picker | Application, filters | Date Picker Dialog (or native input — decision in Phase A) |
| S7 | Session timeout warning modal | All authenticated | Modal/Alert Dialog (focus trap + restoration) |
| S8 | Skip link / landmark structure | All | Bypass Blocks |

#### Tier 3 — PUBLIC CONTENT (sample + automated-heavy, light manual)
Mostly static prose. Highest-value checks are automated (contrast, alt text, heading order, link text). Manual effort is minimal because there are few dynamic interactions once the shared nav/search/alerts (Tier 2) are covered.

Structured sample (test these specific pages):
- Home (entry point; densest shared-component usage)
- One Program Detail variant — **the most complex of the 3 variants** (embedded YouTube lives here → captions/controls)
- FAQ (likely an accordion/disclosure pattern → verify, it may reuse S1's disclosure)
- Contact (form + reCAPTCHA → second 3.3.8 instance)

Random sample (WCAG-EM 3a — guard against the structured sample missing template variance):
- About, News, Program Listings, and the **other 2 Program Detail variants** get an **automated scan + a 10-minute keyboard tab-through** each, but not full SR + full manual. The 2 un-sampled Program Detail variants are checked only for *delta* from the fully-tested variant (do they use the same template? if yes, inherit findings; if no, escalate to full test).

**Sampling sign-off:** WCAG-EM requires recording which pages were sampled and why. This list IS that record and goes verbatim into the VPAT "Evaluation Methods Used" section.

### Page-Count Reconciliation
20 unique pages = 11 Tier-1 + 4 structured-sample Tier-3 + 5 random/delta Tier-3. The 8 shared patterns are tested *orthogonally* (once each, not per-page). This is the only way the arithmetic closes inside 480 hours.

---

## Phase A–F — Shared-Component Correctness Specs (the "what correct looks like" reference)

When an auditor reaches a shared pattern (Tier 2) or encounters it on any page, they test against these specs rather than improvising. This is where the planner's interaction/focus/state phases live. Each entry gives the **APG pattern**, the **must-pass checks**, and the **WCAG criterion** each check defends. An auditor with basic knowledge can execute these as checklists; deviations become findings.

### Phase A — Mega-Menu (S1): Interaction Pattern Decision
**First decide which APG pattern the menu claims to be**, because the correct keyboard model differs:
- If it navigates to URLs (links) → it is a **Disclosure** pattern (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/disclosure/), NOT a menu. Each top-level item is a button with `aria-expanded`; panel contains links. Do **not** expect arrow-key navigation. This is the correct choice for site nav and the most common.
- If it triggers actions/JS commands → **Menubar** (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/menubar/) with full arrow-key roving tabindex.

**Most government nav menus are mislabeled menus that should be disclosures.** A frequent real finding: `role="menu"` applied to a list of links, which then *fails* SR users because arrow keys are now expected but the site only wired click.

Must-pass (assuming Disclosure):
- Trigger is a real `<button>` with `aria-expanded` true/false (SC 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value).
- `aria-controls` points to the panel id (SC 1.3.1 Info and Relationships).
- Both **hover-triggered AND click/focus-triggered** open paths set `aria-expanded` and both close on Escape and on focus-out (SC 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus; SC 2.1.1 Keyboard). *Known pitfall: sites wire the click branch and forget the hover branch's `aria-expanded`.*
- Panel content is keyboard-reachable in DOM order; Escape returns focus to the trigger (SC 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap, 2.4.3 Focus Order).
- `+`/`−`/`▾` glyph indicators wrapped in `aria-hidden="true"` if state is already on `aria-expanded` (SC 4.1.2 — avoid "down-pointing triangle" double-announcement).
- SC 1.4.13: content shown on hover is dismissable, hoverable, persistent.

### Phase B — Search with Live Results (S2): Combobox + Live Region
APG **Combobox** (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/combobox/). Must-pass:
- Input has a **visible `<label>`** plus programmatic association — `aria-label` alone is a finding (known pitfall: container aria-label is not a field label) (SC 1.3.1, 3.3.2 Labels or Instructions).
- `role="combobox"`, `aria-expanded`, `aria-controls`/`aria-activedescendant` to the listbox (SC 4.1.2).
- Down/Up arrow move through results, Enter selects, Escape closes (Combobox APG keyboard model) (SC 2.1.1).
- Result count announced via **one** `aria-live="polite"` / `role="status"` region — "12 results" — that is **not** inside a loop (known pitfall: a `role="status"` per result row spams the SR) (SC 4.1.3 Status Messages).
- No focus stealing: typing must not yank focus to results (SC 2.4.3).

### Phase C — In-Page Alerts & Notification Center (S3): Live Regions
APG **Alert** (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/alert/) for urgent; `role="status"` for non-urgent. Must-pass:
- Urgent errors: `role="alert"` / `aria-live="assertive"`; non-urgent: `role="status"` / `aria-live="polite"` (SC 4.1.3).
- The live region exists in the DOM **before** content is injected (regions added at the same time as their content are unreliably announced) — auditor must confirm in DOM, not just visually (SC 4.1.3).
- **Exactly one** assertive region per event class. Notification center as a feed should use `role="feed"` or a list with `aria-live="polite"`, not N alert roles (known pitfall #1) (SC 4.1.3).
- Color is never the sole signal: error = icon + text + `aria-invalid` on associated field, not just red (SC 1.4.1 Use of Color).

### Phase D — Data Tables (S4): Table Semantics
No APG widget — this is HTML table correctness (SC 1.3.1). Must-pass:
- Real `<table>` > `<thead>` > `<th scope="col">`; **loop-generated identifying cell (application ID / date) is `<th scope="row">`** (known pitfall #6) (SC 1.3.1).
- **Never `role="presentation"` on a data table** (known pitfall #7) (SC 1.3.1).
- Sort controls (if any) are `<button>`s inside the `<th>` with `aria-sort` on the column header (ascending/descending/none), and the sort change announces via a status region (SC 4.1.2, 4.1.3).
- Caption or `aria-labelledby` names the table (SC 1.3.1).

### Phase E — File Upload (S5) & Date Picker (S6)
**File upload** — native `<input type="file">` is the accessible baseline. Must-pass:
- A real labeled file input exists even if visually a styled dropzone sits on top (SC 1.3.1, 4.1.2).
- Drag-drop is an **enhancement, never the only path** — there must be a click/Enter file picker (SC 2.5.7 Dragging Movements, 2.1.1 Keyboard).
- Upload progress and success/failure announced via `role="status"` and the file's a11y name updates; errors associate to the input via `aria-describedby` + `aria-invalid` (SC 4.1.3, 3.3.1 Error Identification).

**Date picker** — decide native vs. custom:
- Native `<input type="date">` = accessible baseline, strongly preferred for a benefits form. If custom, it must follow APG **Date Picker Dialog** (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/date-picker-dialog/): grid role, arrow-key day navigation, focus trapped in dialog, focus restored to the trigger on close, current date communicated (SC 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.4.3, 4.1.2). A text input alternative accepting typed dates must exist regardless (SC 3.3.2 — format instructions associated via `aria-describedby`).

### Phase F — Session Timeout Modal (S7) & Skip Link/Landmarks (S8)
**Session timeout modal** — APG **Alert Dialog** (https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/alertdialog/). This is the single highest-risk shared component for this population (a benefits applicant timing out mid-application and losing data). Must-pass:
- `role="alertdialog"`, `aria-labelledby` + `aria-describedby` (SC 4.1.2, 1.3.1).
- Focus **moves into** the dialog on open and is **trapped** (Tab cycles within) (SC 2.1.2, APG Alert Dialog).
- On dismiss ("Stay signed in") focus **restores to where the user was**, not to top of page (SC 2.4.3).
- **SC 2.2.1 Timing Adjustable** is the governing criterion: the user must be able to extend, and the warning must appear with enough time to react (≥20 seconds before timeout per 2.2.1). This is a content/behavior finding, not just an ARIA finding — verify the *timing*, not only the markup.
- SC 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured: confirm the modal does not get hidden behind a sticky header.

**Skip link + landmarks** — APG **Bypass Blocks** / landmark practice. Must-pass:
- First focusable element is a skip-to-main link, visible on focus (two-color focus ring) (SC 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks).
- One `<main>`, named `<nav>`s, `<header>`/`<footer>` landmarks; headings h1→h2→h3 with no skips (SC 1.3.1, 2.4.6 Headings and Labels).
- Reverse skip-link / "back to top" on long content pages (SC 2.4.1).

### Cross-Cutting Visual & Content Checks (apply to every page, mostly automatable)
From planner Phases 6–7, run on every sampled page:
- **Contrast** 4.5:1 text / 3:1 large+non-text (SC 1.4.3, 1.4.11) — axe catches most; manual-verify gradients/overlays/text-on-image axe can't compute.
- **Color not sole indicator** (SC 1.4.1); **links in prose underlined / 3:1 vs body text** (SC 1.4.1).
- **Reflow** at 320px / 400% zoom, no horizontal scroll, no lost functionality (SC 1.4.10).
- **Resize text** to 200% (SC 1.4.4); text-spacing override survives (SC 1.4.12).
- **Reduced motion** honored (SC 2.3.3); the analytics/3rd-party scripts don't inject motion.
- **Target size** 24×24 CSS px minimum (SC 2.5.8 — WCAG 2.2 AA figure).
- **Two-color focus indicator**, `:focus-visible`, 3:1 (SC 2.4.7, 2.4.11).
- **Alt text**: decorative `alt=""`, informative concise, **image-in-link with `aria-label`/decorative link → `alt=""`** so the decorative description doesn't fight the link purpose (known pitfall #8) (SC 1.1.1).
- **Accessible names via visible label or `aria-label`, never `title`** (known pitfall #2) (SC 4.1.2).
- **Lang attributes** `<html lang>` + per-phrase `lang` (SC 3.1.1, 3.1.2) — relevant for a benefits portal likely offering Spanish.

---

## Tool Selection Rationale (WCAG-EM Step 2 tooling)

These tools are **complementary, not substitutes.** Treating axe and WAVE as interchangeable (a "below expectations" anti-pattern) misunderstands what each does. The division of labor:

| Tool | What it is good at | What it CANNOT do | Role in this audit |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **axe DevTools Enterprise** (licensed) | Rule-based DOM analysis; high precision (near-zero false positives); scriptable in CI via `@axe-core/playwright`; catches contrast, missing alt, ARIA misuse, label association, heading order, region/landmark issues | Cannot judge focus management, live-region *announcement*, keyboard operability of dynamic widgets, meaningfulness of alt text, reading order intent, timing (2.2.1). Catches ~30–40% of WCAG issues by criterion. | **Primary automated layer.** Run on every sampled page + every state variant. Authoritative for the criteria it covers. |
| **WAVE** (browser extension) | *Visual overlay* on the rendered page — shows landmarks, heading outline, contrast, ARIA, alt text inline. Excellent for **manual reviewers** to *see* structure at a glance. | Not scriptable; not for CI; overlaps axe on rules but its value is the human-facing overlay, not the rule engine. | **Manual-review aid.** Used by the engineer during keyboard/visual passes to visualize structure and spot-confirm landmarks/headings. Complement to axe, not a second axe. |
| **Lighthouse** (Chrome) | Bundles axe-core a subset + perf/SEO; one-click; good for a **fast triage smoke test** and for non-experts. | Subset of axe's rules; not the authoritative a11y engine when full axe is licensed; perf signals are tangential to WCAG. | **Triage only** — a quick pass during Phase 2 recon to flag obviously broken pages. Not a conformance source; axe Enterprise supersedes it. |
| **Manual keyboard testing** | The **only** way to verify dynamic interaction: focus order, traps, restoration, arrow-key widget operation, Escape behavior, the timeout modal, the date picker, the combobox. | Slow; requires the per-pattern specs (Phases A–F) to be repeatable across two engineers. | **Mandatory for all Tier-1 + Tier-2.** Highest-value human hours. |
| **Screen reader testing** | Human judgment on *announcement* — name/role/state spoken correctly, live updates heard, errors associated, reading order sensible. | Slowest; SR-specific quirks (JAWS vs NVDA vs VO) require the SR matrix to avoid combinatorial explosion. | **Mandatory for Tier-1 + Tier-2**, sampled for Tier-3. |
| **DOM inspection** (DevTools) | Confirms `aria-*` attributes land on the *correct* element and references resolve (id targets exist). | N/A | **Required verification step** for every ARIA finding (known pitfall #9 — visual/automated pass is not enough). |

**Rule:** axe says PASS ≠ accessible. axe says FAIL = real (near-zero false positives) and is citable directly. A clean axe scan only retires ~⅓ of the criteria; the rest are earned by keyboard + SR + DOM inspection.

### axe DevTools Enterprise Configuration
- **Rule sets enabled:** `wcag2a`, `wcag2aa`, `wcag21a`, `wcag21aa`, `wcag22aa`, plus `best-practice` (best-practice findings map to VPAT ADVISORY, not conformance failures — keep them separated in reporting).
- **Section 508 tag** enabled so findings map to the 508 columns the VPAT needs.
- **Exclusions (scoped, documented):** Exclude third-party iframes from *remediation* scans but **scan them separately for documentation** — the reCAPTCHA widget, the embedded YouTube `<iframe>`, and the analytics-injected nodes get their own report whose findings become VPAT "Not Evaluated / Third-Party" notes, not failures the team must fix. Document every exclusion selector in the audit log (WCAG-EM reproducibility).
- **State variants:** axe scans the *current* DOM, so script each page into each meaningful state before scanning — form **empty / filled / error-validated**; mega-menu **open**; search **with results**; modal **open**; table **sorted**. A scan of only the default state misses the error and open states where most ARIA lives.
- **Run modes:** (1) interactive axe DevTools during manual review for the engineer; (2) `@axe-core/playwright` in a scripted suite for reproducibility and re-test after fixes.

---

## Testing Matrix (which methods apply to which tier)

| Tier | Pages/Patterns | axe (auto) | Lighthouse triage | Keyboard | Screen reader | WAVE overlay | DOM inspect | Visual/zoom |
|------|----------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **T1 Critical path** | Acct, login, 6 app steps, upload, dashboard, notifications | ✅ all states | — | ✅ full | ✅ full (per SR matrix) | ✅ | ✅ all ARIA | ✅ |
| **T2 Shared components** | S1–S8 | ✅ all states | — | ✅ full | ✅ full | ✅ | ✅ all ARIA | ✅ |
| **T3 structured sample** | Home, top Program Detail (+YouTube), FAQ, Contact | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ tab-through | ✅ partial | ✅ | ✅ key ARIA | ✅ |
| **T3 random/delta** | About, News, Listings, 2 other Program Details | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ 10-min tab | — (inherit if same template) | optional | — | spot |
| **3rd-party** | reCAPTCHA, YouTube, analytics | ✅ separate report | — | ✅ operability only | ✅ operability only | — | — | — |

---

## Screen Reader Test Matrix (which SR covers which page type)

5 SRs × 20 pages is infeasible. WCAG-EM permits testing with a representative AT set. Assignment principle: **cover each rendering engine/SR pairing that real users use, distribute pages so every Tier-1 flow is hit by ≥2 SRs across the desktop/mobile divide, and put mobile SRs on the flows where mobile usage is highest (application + status, because applicants check status on phones).**

| Screen reader | Browser/OS pairing | Assigned coverage | Rationale |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| **NVDA** | Firefox / Windows | All Tier-1 flows + S1,S2,S3,S7; all structured-sample T3 | Free, most common combined desktop SR usage; the default workhorse |
| **JAWS** | Chrome / Windows | Application 6 steps, login, dashboard, S4 tables, S6 date picker | Dominant in government/enterprise; differs from NVDA on tables and forms — the exact Tier-1 surfaces |
| **VoiceOver** | Safari / macOS | S1 nav, S2 search, S5 upload, Program Detail (+YouTube), Contact | Only SR that exposes Safari/WebKit quirks; covers shared nav + media |
| **iOS VoiceOver** | Safari / iOS | Login, application steps 1–3, document upload, **application status dashboard**, notification center | Mobile-first applicant journey; touch + rotor; status-on-phone is the real use case |
| **TalkBack** | Chrome / Android | Account creation, application steps 4–6, status dashboard, session timeout modal | Android share of low-income mobile users is high; verifies the timeout modal on touch |

Every Tier-1 flow is covered by at least one desktop SR **and** at least one mobile SR. Tables get JAWS (its weak spot historically). The timeout modal gets both VO-mobile and TalkBack (touch focus-trap behavior differs from desktop).

---

## Prioritization Framework (by USER IMPACT, not WCAG number)

Ranking by criterion number (e.g., "all 1.x before 2.x") is an explicit anti-pattern — it sorts by spec accident, not human harm. The framework sorts by **whether a person can complete their journey**:

| Severity | Definition | Examples for this portal | VPAT effect |
|----------|------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **CRITICAL** | **Blocks completion of a user journey** — no workaround. A citizen cannot apply, log in, upload a required document, or read their decision. | Keyboard trap in application step 3; submit button unreachable; reCAPTCHA with no accessible alternative (SC 3.3.8) blocking login; timeout modal that can't be dismissed by keyboard; file upload with drag-only path. | "Does Not Support" on the affected criterion; **gates the VPAT** — agency should not publish a conformance claim while CRITICALs are open on the critical path. |
| **MAJOR** | **Significant friction, but a workaround exists.** Journey completable with extra effort/expert knowledge. | Form errors not associated to fields (user can still find them visually); table missing row headers (navigable but disorienting); search results count not announced; missing skip link. | "Partially Supports" with remarks. |
| **MINOR** | **Inconvenience**; does not impede completion. | Decorative icon announced redundantly; non-underlined link in prose where color contrast is otherwise sufficient; heading-level skip in footer. | "Partially Supports" or noted remark. |
| **ADVISORY** | **Best practice / robustness**, not a 2.2 AA failure. | axe `best-practice` rule hits; AAA enhancements; recommendations beyond conformance. | Not a conformance failure; optional "Additional Information" note. |

Severity is assigned by **impact on the population**, validated at the a11y-critic gate. A MAJOR that happens to sit on the only path to benefits gets escalated to CRITICAL — context overrides the generic label (this matches the repo's "severity reflects impact on people, not rule weight" principle).

**Remediation sequencing falls out of this:** fix CRITICALs on Tier-1 first; then Tier-2 shared-component CRITICAL/MAJOR (one fix, 20-page payoff); then Tier-1 MAJOR; then the rest.

---

## Third-Party Component Handling (VPAT-critical — legal accuracy)

Omitting this produces a **legally incorrect VPAT.** Third-party components you do not control cannot be *required* to be remediated, but they **must be documented** and their impact disclosed. Under the VPAT/508 model and WCAG conformance, you evaluate them, you disclose conformance honestly, and you note they are third-party and what mitigations exist.

| Component | Test | VPAT treatment | Mitigation expectation |
|-----------|------|----------------|------------------------|
| **reCAPTCHA v2** (login, contact) | Operability via keyboard + each SR; check the audio-challenge fallback and any "accessible reCAPTCHA" path | **SC 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication (Minimum)** risk: a cognitive image puzzle is exactly what 3.3.8 targets. Likely "Partially Supports" / "Does Not Support" with a remark identifying it as a third-party Google component. | Recommend reCAPTCHA's audio alternative is enabled, or switch to reCAPTCHA v3 / a non-cognitive alternative (email link, passkey). Document that an **alternative authentication method exists** — required to even claim Partial. If login is *only* reachable via an inaccessible CAPTCHA, that is CRITICAL regardless of third-party status, because no alternative = blocked journey. |
| **Embedded YouTube** (program pages) | Captions present + accurate (SC 1.2.2 Captions); player controls keyboard-operable + SR-labeled (SC 2.1.1, 4.1.2); no autoplay-with-sound (SC 1.4.2) | YouTube player chrome is third-party (document its control a11y as-is). **Caption presence/accuracy is the agency's content responsibility, not third-party** — uncaptioned program videos are a genuine agency failure under 1.2.2, not excusable as "YouTube's fault." | Ensure every embedded video has accurate (not auto-generated) captions + a transcript link. Audio description (1.2.5 AA) if videos convey visual-only info. |
| **Third-party analytics script** | Confirm it injects no focusable/visible UI, no motion, no live-region noise, no focus stealing | Usually invisible → "Supports" / "Not Applicable" *if* it adds no UI. Verify it doesn't inject a cookie/consent banner that obscures focus (SC 2.4.11) or trap focus. | If it injects a consent banner, that banner is in-scope and tested like any modal. |

**VPAT language pattern for third-party:** "Partially Supports. This functionality is provided by a third-party component ([name]). [Conformance observation]. An alternative accessible method ([describe]) is available." Never silently mark third-party components "Supports" — and never omit them.

---

## VPAT Reporting Structure & Conformance Claims

The audit's entire output is a VPAT; a plan that doesn't connect to it is incomplete. Target template: **ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508** (the 508 edition, since this is a US government portal — it carries the Revised Section 508 chapters, which incorporate WCAG 2.x by reference).

### VPAT structure to populate
1. **Product/version, evaluation date, evaluator(s), evaluation methods** → cite **WCAG-EM 1.0** as the methodology, list the sampled pages (the Phase 0 list verbatim), list the AT/tools used (the SR matrix + tool table).
2. **Conformance-level table per criterion** with the four standard claims:
   - **Supports** — meets the criterion across the evaluated scope.
   - **Partially Supports** — some functionality meets it, some does not (most MAJOR/MINOR land here, with remarks).
   - **Does Not Support** — fails (CRITICALs).
   - **Not Applicable** — criterion doesn't apply (e.g., 1.2.x if there were no media — but here there IS media, so 1.2.x applies).
   - (**Not Evaluated** is permitted only for the AAA chapter, which a 2.2 AA VPAT need not evaluate.)
3. **Per-criterion Remarks and Explanations** — every Partial/Does-Not-Support needs a specific, factual remark (the finding text + page + the user impact, not the WCAG number alone).
4. **Tables/Forms/508 chapters** — the 508 functional performance criteria and hardware/software/support-doc chapters as the template requires.

### Conformance-claim discipline
- A claim is only as strong as the sample. State the sample. Do **not** assert site-wide "Supports" from a 20-page sample without the WCAG-EM caveat.
- **Honest "Partially Supports" beats inflated "Supports."** An overclaimed VPAT is the legal liability this whole project exists to avoid.

### Criteria most likely to FAIL on a 4-year-old government site (where to expect findings — pre-load the team's attention)
Based on the common failure distribution for sites of this vintage and type, expect concentrated findings on:
- **1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum)** — perennial #1; brand colors that miss 4.5:1.
- **1.1.1 Non-text Content** — decorative images with verbose alt; icon fonts not `aria-hidden`.
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** — custom widgets (mega-menu, date picker) missing states; `title` used as accessible name.
- **2.4.7 / 2.4.11 Focus** — invisible focus indicators; sticky header obscuring focus (2.4.11 is new in 2.2, almost never satisfied yet).
- **1.3.1 Info and Relationships** — tables without row headers; form labels not associated.
- **3.3.1 / 3.3.3 Error handling** — validation errors not associated/announced (huge for a 6-step benefits form).
- **2.1.1 / 2.1.2 Keyboard** — date picker, drag-only upload, mega-menu hover-only.
- **4.1.3 Status Messages** — live search/alerts/upload status not announced (the JS-enhanced islands).
- **3.3.7 Redundant Entry, 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication, 2.5.8 Target Size, 2.5.7 Dragging** — the new-in-2.2 criteria the old build never targeted.
- **1.2.2 Captions** — the embedded YouTube program videos.

---

## Execution Order & Timeline (3 months, 2 engineers)

WCAG-EM is iterative; this sequences it into the deadline and concentrates the scarce SR/manual hours on Tier-1/Tier-2.

| Phase | Weeks | Work | Engineer split |
|-------|-------|------|----------------|
| **0. Setup & recon** | 1 | WCAG-EM Step 1–2; finalize sample list; configure axe Enterprise rule sets + exclusions; build Playwright state-driver scripts; Lighthouse triage smoke of all 20 pages | E1 axe/Playwright config; E2 recon + sample finalization |
| **1. Automated sweep** | 2 | axe on all 20 pages × all state variants; reCAPTCHA/YouTube/analytics scanned separately; log findings into the tracker with WCAG tags | E1 critical-path + shared; E2 public pages + 3rd-party |
| 🔍 **Gate 1** | end wk2 | a11y-critic on automated findings: false-positive triage, severity calibration, coverage-gap check (what axe *can't* see) | — |
| **2. Manual keyboard** | 3–4 | Tier-1 + Tier-2 full keyboard (Phases A–F specs); Tier-3 tab-throughs; DOM-inspect every ARIA finding | E1 application flow + modal + date picker; E2 nav + search + tables + upload |
| 🔍 **Gate 2** | end wk4 | a11y-critic on keyboard/focus findings: focus-trap/restoration logic, APG-pattern correctness, severity by journey-impact | — |
| **3. Screen reader** | 5–7 | Execute the SR matrix; record announcements verbatim for each finding; mobile SRs on application/status | E1 NVDA+JAWS (Win); E2 VO+iOS VO+TalkBack |
| 🔍 **Gate 3** | end wk7 | a11y-critic on SR findings: announcement quality, live-region behavior, mobile-SR parity, dead-finding check (is each finding a real user-experienced problem?) | — |
| **4. Visual/zoom/reflow** | 8 | 200%/400%, reflow at 320px, contrast manual-verify (overlays/gradients axe missed), reduced-motion, target size | both |
| **5. VPAT drafting** | 9–10 | Map every finding → criterion → conformance claim + remark; write Evaluation Methods (cite WCAG-EM + sample + tools); assemble ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508 | E1 criterion tables; E2 remarks + methods narrative |
| 🔍 **Gate 4** | end wk10 | a11y-critic on VPAT draft: claim accuracy (no overclaiming), remark specificity, third-party language correctness, sample disclosure, CRITICAL-gating check | — |
| **6. Remediation re-test buffer** | 11–12 | Re-run `@axe-core/playwright` + targeted keyboard/SR on fixed CRITICALs; update VPAT claims; final sign-off | both |

The buffer (wks 11–12) exists because some CRITICALs *will* be fixed before publication (esp. shared-component CRITICALs with 20-page payoff), and the VPAT must reflect the as-published state, re-tested under the same conditions (evidence-gated — no claim flips to "Supports" without before/after proof on the same page/AT).

---

## Acceptance Criteria (what "audit complete" means)

The audit is done when **all** are true:
1. Every Tier-1 page and every Tier-2 shared pattern has: an axe scan (all states), a full keyboard pass against its Phase A–F spec, an SR pass per the matrix, and DOM verification of every ARIA finding.
2. Every Tier-3 sampled page has an axe scan + keyboard tab-through; delta pages confirmed same-template or escalated.
3. Every finding is logged with: page/component, WCAG criterion, severity (by user impact), reproduction steps, the SR announcement (for SR findings), and a DOM note confirming attribute placement.
4. Third-party components (reCAPTCHA, YouTube, analytics) are evaluated and documented with VPAT third-party language; the YouTube caption check is treated as an agency content responsibility.
5. The prioritization framework is applied; no CRITICAL on the critical path is silently downgraded.
6. The ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508 is populated with per-criterion claims, specific remarks, a WCAG-EM methodology statement, and the sample list.
7. All four a11y-critic gates passed (or findings dispositioned).
8. The VPAT does not claim "Supports" beyond what the sample + re-test evidence support.

---

## a11y-Critic Review Checkpoints

| Checkpoint | After Phase | Critic focus |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| 🔍 1 | Automated sweep (wk2) | False-positive triage; **coverage gaps** (enumerate what axe structurally cannot catch so manual phases target them); severity calibration; confirm state-variant scans actually ran |
| 🔍 2 | Manual keyboard (wk4) | Focus-trap/restoration correctness on modal + date picker; APG-pattern match (is the mega-menu correctly a Disclosure, not a mislabeled menu?); keyboard-trap CRITICALs; severity-by-journey-impact |
| 🔍 3 | Screen reader (wk7) | Announcement quality (name/role/state actually spoken); live-region behavior (one region, fires reliably, not in a loop); **mobile-SR parity** with desktop; dead-finding check — is each finding something a real user experiences, or a checklist artifact? |
| 🔍 4 | VPAT draft (wk10) | **No overclaiming**; every Partial/Does-Not-Support has a specific user-impact remark (not a bare WCAG number); third-party language legally correct; sample disclosed; CRITICALs gate the conformance claim; methods cite WCAG-EM |

---

### Contract Appendix (for spec-kitty-bridge WP translation)

#### Architecture Overview
A WCAG-EM-grounded, risk-tiered site audit. Three test tiers (Critical path / Shared components / Public content) crossed with a complementary tool stack (axe Enterprise authoritative-automated, WAVE/Lighthouse human-aid/triage, keyboard+SR+DOM for everything automation can't see). Shared-component leverage (test once, fix once, propagate to 20 pages) is the efficiency mechanism that fits the audit in 3 months. Output is an ITTF VPAT 2.4 Rev 508 whose claims are evidence-gated and sample-disclosed.

#### Implementation Tasks
- **Task 0 — Setup & recon.** Effort: medium. Depends on: none. Configure axe Enterprise (rule sets `wcag2a/aa`, `wcag21aa`, `wcag22aa`, `best-practice`, 508 tag; documented third-party exclusions; state-driver scripts). Finalize the WCAG-EM sample list. Lighthouse triage smoke of 20 pages.
- **Task 1 — Automated sweep.** Effort: medium. Depends on: 0. axe all 20 pages × all states; separate third-party scans.
- **Task 2 — Manual keyboard.** Effort: high. Depends on: 1. Tier-1+Tier-2 against Phase A–F specs; DOM-inspect ARIA.
- **Task 3 — Screen reader.** Effort: high. Depends on: 2. Execute SR matrix incl. mobile SRs.
- **Task 4 — Visual/zoom/reflow.** Effort: low. Depends on: 1.
- **Task 5 — VPAT drafting.** Effort: medium. Depends on: 1,2,3,4. Findings → claims → remarks; methods narrative.
- **Task 6 — Remediation re-test buffer.** Effort: medium. Depends on: 5. Re-test fixed CRITICALs under same conditions; update claims.

#### Test Strategy per Task
- Task 1: axe PASS retires only its ~⅓ of criteria; everything else flows to 2/3. Re-runnable via `@axe-core/playwright`.
- Task 2: each finding gets reproduction steps + DOM note (attribute on correct element, id target resolves).
- Task 3: each SR finding records the verbatim announcement and the SR/browser pairing.
- Task 5/6: no claim reaches "Supports" without before/after evidence on the same page + AT.

#### Acceptance Criteria per Task
Derived from the master Acceptance Criteria list above; per task = that task's slice (e.g., Task 3 acceptance = SR matrix fully executed, every Tier-1 flow hit by ≥1 desktop + ≥1 mobile SR, announcements recorded).

### Failure Modes
- **Sampling everything equally** → blows the 3-month budget and still under-tests the critical path. Mitigation: the 3-tier model.
- **Treating axe and WAVE as substitutes** → false confidence; ~⅔ of criteria unexamined. Mitigation: the tool-role table; axe-clean ≠ accessible.
- **Omitting/overclaiming third-party** → legally incorrect VPAT. Mitigation: separate third-party scan + mandatory third-party VPAT language + the "no alternative = CRITICAL" override.
- **Severity by WCAG number** → wrong remediation order. Mitigation: impact-based framework + critic gate.
- **Desktop-only SR matrix** → misses the mobile-majority applicant journey. Mitigation: iOS VO + TalkBack assigned to application/status flows.
- **Findings disconnected from the VPAT** → audit produces a pile of bugs, not a conformance document. Mitigation: every finding maps to a criterion + claim + remark; VPAT drafting is a named phase with its own critic gate.
- **Dead findings** ("missing aria-label" with no user-experience explanation) → checklist theater. Mitigation: critic Gate 3 dead-finding check; SR findings must carry the actual announcement.
